title: Goal Setting and Saving in the FinTech Era
abstract: We study the effectiveness of saving goals in increasing individuals' savings using data from a Fintech app. Using a difference-in-differences identification strategy that randomly assigns users into a group of beta testers who can set goals and a group of users who cannot, we find that setting goals increases individuals' savings rate. The increased savings within the app do not reduce savings outside the app. Moreover, goal setting helps those individuals previously identified as having the lowest propensity to save. Matching App user survey responses to their behavior highlights the relative merits of monitoring and concreteness channels in explaining our findings.

article.introduction:
“Ignoranti quem portum petat nullus suus ventus est”
  “If one does not know to which port one is sailing, no wind is favorable”
  The economics and finance literaturehas uncovered several behavioral biases as the main psychological reasons a large majority of individuals do not save enough to withstand adverse income shocks and are unprepared for retirement.1Much less is known, however, about how to correct these biases on a large scale.2In this paper, we provide evidence that financial technology (FinTech) apps can help individuals improve their consumption-savings choices by helping them set saving goals.
  Goals differ in terms of whether individuals can deviate from them—hardversussoft. Hard goals generally entail penalties for not following the planned course of action, such as withdrawing funds from a savings account before a preestablished horizon (Ashraf, Karlan, and Yin (2006), Karlan et al. (2016)). Less is known about soft goals (Beshears, Milkman, and Schwartzstein (2016)), whereby individuals face no penalties when deviating from their plan. The key trade-off between the two is that soft goals are more scalable and easier to administer compared to hard goals, especially in real-life settings. At the same time, the lack of monetary costs associated with noncompliance may make soft goals less effective (DellaVigna and Malmendier (2006)).3
  We study the effectiveness of soft goals using data from a FinTech app that provides its users with simple budgeting solutions in the form of saving goals. Assessing this question has important policy implications as a large number of banks and FinTech firms are now offering goal-setting tools for saving to their customers—see FigureIA.1for some examples.4
  Our setting is particularly suited for measuring the effect of saving goals uncontaminated by other confounding factors as the app did not include over our sample period reminders, notifications, automatic saving plans, or other nudges that have been shown to be effective in promoting saving.5To distinguish among the various economic channels underlying the effectiveness of goal setting, we complement the app data with a survey administered to a sample of app users (Liu et al. (2022)).
  The psychological literature highlights that the effectiveness of goal setting hinges on the appropriateness and correct design of the goal—see Locke and Latham (2002), Locke and Latham (2006). Accordingly, we start with a characterization of the goals set by app users and assess the extent to which they are realistic and congruent with finance theory. To create a saving goal, users have to decide the amount, horizon, purpose, and the investment vehicle associated with it.6We find that the vast majority of the goals have horizons of less than one year and are less than € 2,500. Half of the goals do not belong to a specific category and are assigned by the users to the generic “Savings” category. Among the specific categories, “Travel” is the most common, comprising 20% of the goals, followed by “Car,” “Hobby,” and “House,” each comprising less than 10% of the goals. In terms of investment vehicle, Balanced funds are the most common choice (comprising 55% of goals), followed by Fixed Income (32%) and Equity funds (13%). Both nonparametric and regression results indicate a positive relation between both goal horizon and amount on the one hand, and the risk of the chosen investment fund on the other hand, consistent with the view that users understand the time value of money and perceive stocks as a better investment in the longer run. In contrast, we do not detect a significant relationship between risk-taking and the purpose of the goal.
  We next examine the dynamics of goal creation and achievement. The practice of setting saving goals is quite widespread and persistent. About 41% of users create at least one goal, and they are slightly more likely to do so right after they start using the app. Moreover, users do not stop using goals once they start setting them. At the same time, only a small fraction of users set multiple saving goals simultaneously. Turning to goal achievement, unattained goals are very different from those attained from their inception. In the former case, individuals tend to undersave from the very beginning, while in the latter case individuals oversave at first and then progressively decrease their savings rate, only to increase it at the very end of the goal's life to meet the goal. Goals with a shorter horizon and smaller size have greater completion probability, while all of the specific goal categories have a probability of achievement significantly lower than the generic Savings category.
  Our central findings speak to the effect of goal setting on saving behavior. Our setting allows us to identify causal effects using a quasi-natural experiment. The app developers activated the goal-setting feature for 122 randomly chosen individuals (beta testers) 50 days before releasing it to the whole population. This allows us to estimate a difference-in-differences model in which the beta testers comprise the treated group and the rest of the users comprise the control group. Because the users in the treated group had access to the goal-setting feature but were not forced to use it, with this identification strategy we can estimate an intention to treat (ITT) effect—the effect of deploying goal setting on the broader population at large—as well as a local average treatment effect (LATE)—the effect of goal setting for those who actually set goals. We estimate an ITT of between € 16.92 and € 20.01 per month and an LATE of € 59.57 per month. The magnitude of these effects survives when we combine the difference-in-differences procedure with propensity-score matching (Chabé-Ferret (2017)). Finally, we find no effect of goal setting on saving when we recompute our results using two pseudo treatment windows in the immediate proximity of the true one, further validating our identification strategy.
  While our difference-in-differences setting has the appealing aspect of establishing causality, it provides limited insights into the dynamics of goal setting and saving because the beta-testing period spanned only two months. Moreover, the number of beta testers does not provide enough power to explore heterogeneities across users' characteristics. To overcome these limitations, we also provide results that use the full sample of users and estimate panel regressions that control for individual-specific and time fixed effects. Individuals save an additional € 28.74 per month, on average, when they engage in goal setting. Before using the goal-setting feature, the same individuals were saving € 28.75 per month on the app, suggesting that goal setting doubles individuals' savings. Economically, these effects are remarkable, given that—as we stress above—the app does not include reminders, notifications, automatic saving plans, or other saving nudges. When we decompose the overall savings into withdrawals and deposits, we find the additional savings are driven mainly by an increase in the deposit rate rather than a decrease in the withdrawal rate.
  We first augment these results with dynamic specifications that study the effectiveness of goal setting over time. Users save substantially more in the first month after adopting goal setting, where additional savings exceed € 45 per month. Over time, however, the additional savings stabilize to approximately € 10 to € 20 per month. This indicates that the initial enthusiasm induced by the new saving tool explains part, but not all, of the savings differential we document in the baseline results.
  We next study heterogeneous effects with respect to the individual characteristics the literature has found to drive undersaving. The most common reasons behind individuals' inability to save range from low levels of financial literacy (Lusardi (2008), Lusardi and Mitchell (2014)), to high impatience (Laibson (1997)), low levels of education (Cole, Paulson, and Shastry (2014)), low income and socioeconomic status (Duflo and Banerjee (2011), Kuhnen and Miu (2017)), and inattention (Karlan et al. (2016)). We find that even those individuals potentially more prone to undersaving significantly increase their saving after engaging in goal setting.
  We conclude this part of the analysis by ruling out that the increased savings we observe inside the app are a substitute for savings outside the app, and by assessing the economic magnitude of goal setting relative to users' income. To this end, we combine survey and administrative data. The survey explicitly asks app users how their saving habits outside the app changed after its adoption and shows that, if anything, they have increased their savings outside the app. We also reestimate our baseline findings on those individuals who declare not saving outside the app and show that the results are very similar to those for the overall population. To better understand the economic magnitude associated with goal setting, we focus on the subset of app users for which we have income information. Our results show that the median user increases her savings rate from 1% before using goals to 2.4% of her net income when using goals, suggesting an economically large effect of goal setting on saving.
  In the final part of the paper, we use both survey and administrative data to assess the relative merits of different economic mechanisms in explaining the effectiveness of goal setting on saving (Beshears, Milkman, and Schwartzstein (2016)). We first consider a monitoring channel whereby goal setting increases savings because individuals experience disutility from falling short of their goal (Bénabou and Tirole (2004), Hsiaw (2013)) and/or goals help individuals to follow through on their intentions by increasing their attention (Karlan et al. (2016)). We also explore a goal concreteness channel whereby the specific purpose of the goal creates a specific segregated mental account that acts as a saving motivator (Thaler (1985), Shefrin and Thaler (1988), Thaler (1990), Thaler (1999)). A large psychology literature also establishes that setting explicit and concrete goals increases achievement, even in the absence of external incentives (Locke and Latham (1990)).
  When surveyed, 57% of app users responded that the ability to monitor their progress toward the goal was the most motivating aspect of having a goal, followed by the purpose of the goal (28.5%) and having a deadline (10.4%). When asked about the factors that they believe determined the attainment of their goals, 54% of app users highlighted the importance of fund returns and their ability to monitor their progress, followed by 18% (14%) highlighting the importance of the purpose of the goal (having a deadline).
  We use observational data to relate users' saving behavior to the characteristics of the goals they set, such as type, horizon, and goal amount. When we group goals based on type (leisure, which includes Hobby and Travel;durable, which includes Car and House; andother), we find that specific goals are associated with lower savings than generic goals. This result survives when we create goal categories using the textual description of the goal typed by the users at the time of its creation, creating either a restrictive “Savings” category that only includes goals with the lowest likelihood of being specific or recategorizing goals manually into 12 categories.7Overall, the results indicate that both mechanisms are at play, with greater emphasis on a monitoring channel compared to a goal-specificity channel.
  The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we discuss the related literature. In SectionII, we summarize the setting and the data. In SectionIII, we provide stylized facts about how app users set goals. In SectionIV, we estimate the causal effect of goal setting on saving behavior. In SectionV, we provide additional results on the relation between goal setting and saving. In SectionVI, we focus on the economic channel driving our results. Finally, in SectionVII, we conclude.
article.I. Related Literature:
This paper contributes to the recent and fast-growing literature on financial technology (FinTech) and household welfare. FinTech encompasses a broad range of new technologies that seek to improve and automate the delivery and use of financial services (see Das (2019) for a review of the literature). A recent strand of the literature shows that FinTech can help households improve their trading performance and asset allocation (e.g., Gargano and Rossi (2018), D'Acunto, Prabhala, and Rossi (2019), Rossi and Utkus (2020), D'Acunto and Rossi (2021)), to reduce overspending (D'Acunto, Rossi, and Weber (2019)), to better conduct house searches (Gargano, Giacoletti, and Jarnecic (2023)) and to save on bank fees (D'Acunto et al. (2019), Carlin, Olafsson, and Pagel (2020), Loh and Choi (2020)).8At the same time, another strand of the literature highlights the pitfalls generated by the introduction of new technologies. For example, Fuster et al. (2022) find that Black and Hispanic borrowers are disproportionately less likely to gain from the introduction of machine learning tools to predict creditworthiness, while Di Maggio and Yao (2021) find that FinTech borrowers are significantly more likely to default than their peers borrowing from traditional financial institutions. Laudenbach, Pirschel, and Siegel (2018) find that borrowers who speak directly with a bank agent are significantly less likely to default. We contribute to this literature by showing the benefits of FinTech in the savings domain, which remains largely unexplored.
  We also contribute to the literature that studies the effectiveness of saving goals.9A first strand of the literature analyzes goals as a tool to correct self-control problems. Ashraf, Karlan, and Yin (2006) run an experiment in the Philippines and show that individuals with higher hyperbolic discounting are more likely to open a savings product that restricts access to deposits unless the users achieve their saving goal, which in turn increases their savings. John (2020) also uses an experimental setting in the Philippines but shows that imperfect knowledge about their preferences leads individuals to select incentive-incompatible saving commitments, which reduce their welfare. A second strand of the literature analyzes goals as a tool to correct limited attention. Karlan et al. (2016) provide experimental evidence from the Philippines, Bolivia, and Peru showing that reminders increase the likelihood of meeting saving goals and that messages mentioning the nature of the saving goal in addition to the rate incentives are the most effective. In contrast, reminders that mention only the saving goal are largely ineffective. Breza and Chandrasehkar (2019) show that, in India, individuals save more when information about the progress toward the saving goal that they set is shared with another member of the village. Our contribution to this literature is twofold. First, our FinTech setting gives us access to a large sample of individuals coupled with rich administrative data from a developed country where lack of income or access to savings accounts are not the most relevant constraints individuals face. Second, the goals we analyze are not hard commitments since they do not entail a monetary penalty for deviating from the plan, nor do they restrict the set of future available options. These goals are more scalable since they have a higher take-up rate compared to hard goals, but the lack of monetary costs associated with noncompliance may make them less effective (DellaVigna and Malmendier (2006)).
article.II. The Setting and the Data:
This section describes the app and the survey conducted on a random sample of its users. We then describe the main data sources and report summary statistics for the key variables of interest.
  The data used in this study were shared by the Italian asset manager AcomeA, which developed a saving app called Gimme5. Launched in 2014, it was initially designed as a digital piggybank that allowed its users to save small sums of money, such as 5 euros, at a time—hence the name Gimme5. At the time of sign-up, individuals face zero activation costs and no annual fees.10After signing up, users can start saving by depositing as much money as they wish, whenever they want, much like a checking/savings account. These deposits come from the bank account designated by the users when creating their profiles. Unlike checking/savings accounts, however, app users invest their savings in several investment funds that vary in their risk-return profiles. These savings are highly liquid since they can be transformed into cash based on the net asset value of the fund computed at the end of the trading day, as is common practice for mutual funds. As of 2019, individuals could choose from as many as 14 funds. TableIA.Idisplays the annualized return, volatility, and Sharpe ratio of each fund computed over the January 2015 to December 2019 period.
  In September 2017, the app introduced a goal-setting feature through which users can set one or more saving goals. When setting a goal, users are prompted to choose the goal's objective (categorized into six broad categories: Hobby, Travel, Other, Car, House, and a general Savings category), the horizon, and amount. Figure1presents screenshots of the app over these phases. The first step (Panel A) prompts the user to name the target. The second step (Panel B) asks the user to choose between six categories: Hobby (Tempo Libero), Travel (Viaggi), Other (Altro), Car (Veicoli), House (Casa), and Savings (Risparmio). The third step (Panel C) prompts the user to choose a target amount and a deadline to reach the target. Finally, the fourth step (Panel D) asks the user to associate the goal with an image. The app is by no means unique or specific to the Italian setting. In recent years, many banks, such as Chase, Wells Fargo, and financial aggregators in the United States, such as MINT.com, have implemented saving goal tools that are virtually identical in structure and features to the one we describe here—see FigureIA.1for some examples.
  After setting up the target, the user chooses which mutual fund she wants to invest in. The app features three baseline funds, displayed in Figure2, Panel A. Their names are Prudent (Prudente), Dynamic (Dinamico), and Aggressive (Aggressivo), and they are characterized by increasing levels of risk. The users can choose among 14 total funds by clicking on the link “Full List of Funds” (Elenco Completo dei Fondi).
  After the goal and the fund are chosen, the user can deposit money whenever she likes. The app features an interface that shows the user where she stands in achieving the goal. The screenshots in Panels B and C of Figure2provide an illustrative example. In this example, the user has budgeted € 3,000 for a vacation in the United States and has 568 days to meet the goal. As of the day of the screenshot, the person had saved € 10. The graphics make salient how far the user is from the target. The user can click on the pig icon at the bottom of the screen—referred to as “Joink”—to add funds to the account and reach the goal. When the user clicks the Joink button, she is taken to a different page where she can choose how much to deposit for that goal, the default being € 5.
  Crucially, after the introduction of the goal-setting features, users can choose to continue using the app as a digital piggy bank with no goals, to use only the newly introduced goals, or to use both features simultaneously.
  As we stress in the introduction, an important feature that allows us to identify the effect of goal setting on saving is that at no time during our sample did the App have reminders, notifications, automatic saving plans, or other saving nudges. Some of these features were introduced in 2020, while others were launched in the fall of 2021.
  We complement the administrative data with a survey administered to a sample of app users.11Matching individuals' actions on the app to their survey responses is crucial to uncover the economic mechanism underlying individuals' choices—see Liu et al. (2022).12The survey was administered by email to a random sample of 5,000 app users over the week of March 15 to 21, 2021. The number of respondents was 814, for a response rate of 16.3%.
  The survey comprises four sections. In the first, it elicits information that cannot be observed from the administrative data. For example, it asks app users how much they were saving outside the app before and after its adoption. It also asks them what type of accounts they primarily use to save and invest their wealth, that is, savings accounts, brokerage accounts, retirement accounts, etc.
  The second and third sections focus on individuals' motivations for creating targets within the app and using the Joink feature. More precisely, for those who have created at least one target, the survey asks what they believe is the most motivating aspect of goal setting and the main reasons they did or did not achieve a target in the past. It also elicits the underlying psychological motivation for using the Joink feature.
  In the last section, it measures users' patience, risk-aversion, and financial literacy following standard questions in the literature—see Lusardi and Mitchell (2017), Falk et al. (2018), and D'Acunto, Rossi, and Weber (2019). It also asks respondents for their level of education and net income. FigureIA.2provides a summary of these survey responses.
  The data comprisefive Structured Query Language (SQL) tables namedUsers,Goal Information,Deposits and Withdrawals,Investment Vehicle, andLogin Information. The company anonymized all information to guarantee user privacy.
  Users. This table contains information on the 47,643 users who created a profile on the app since its inception. The main variables contained in this table are the opening and closing dates for every savings account created by each user.13Additional information includes users' age, gender, enrollment date, location of residence, place of birth, and other questions related to Know Your Customer (KYC) compliance.
  Goal Information. This table contains information on all of the goals set by users. For each goal, we observe its inception, deadline, amount, purpose—categorized into six broad categories: Hobby, Holiday, Other, Car, House, and generic Savings—and name.
  Deposits and Withdrawals. This table contains information on all of the deposits and withdrawals associated with each user at the daily frequency. For each transaction, we have information on which account and/or goal it is associated with and the amount withdrawn or deposited.
  Investment Vehicle. This table contains information on the name and identifier of the mutual fund associated with each user.
  Login Information. This table contains login time-stamp information for each app user.
  TableIreports cross-sectional summary statistics, computed in two steps. We first compute the value of each variable at the user level and then report the distribution of the variable across all users. For each variable, we report the number of observations used in the second step of the computation, the mean, the standard deviation, and the 1st, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 99thpercentiles.
  Panel A shows that 81% of the app's users are males, suggesting that women are either less targeted by financial apps or less interested in these tools in Italy. Users are rather young, with an average age of 36, consistent with the sample of FinTech app users in D'Acunto et al. (2020), Becker (2017), and Olafsson and Pagel (2018). Using data from the Survey of Household Income and Wealth run by the Bank of Italy, we find that 71% of Italians with access to financial services are male and have an average age of 57. Finally, in terms of income, the results from the survey we run on the app indicate that half of the respondents have a monthly income in the € 1,000 to € 2,000 bracket, in line with the average value of € 1,397 in the Bank of Italy survey.
  Panel B reports results on app usage. Here, the summary statistics are computed using the 27,439 individuals who have transacted at least once. As of November 2019, when we received the data, the average user had used the app for 11.50 months and deposited € 35.04 per month.
  Panel C reports saving goal statistics. Individuals typically set one or—at most—two targets at a time, the average is 1.43. The average investor has a little less than one target open and the average number of targets closed and not completed is equal to 0.3. Finally, the number of completed targets is 0.28 per user.
article.III. The Anatomy of Saving Goals:
Because users have extensive flexibility in designing their goals, their analysis can offer a window into users' savings preferences and beliefs, and allows us to assess the extent to which their goals are realistic, achievable, and congruent with finance theory. To this end, we first analyze the univariate distributions of the characteristics of the goals set by the app users. We then assess the relationship between the characteristics of the goals and the risk profile of the associated investment vehicles. Finally, we study the dynamics of goal-setting behavior over time.
  The top two panels of Figure3report histograms of the goal horizons. To improve legibility, we split the histogram into two parts; the first includes horizons shorter than 1,000 days, while the second includes horizons longer than 1,000 days. Overall, individuals set saving goals that are rather short-lived. The majority of the goals have horizons of less than one year, and a large number of goals have horizons of precisely one year. In addition, we observe clear bunching of the distribution at the one-, two-, three-, to the 10-year horizon.
  The middle two panels of Figure3report histograms of goal amounts. As can be seen, individuals set goal amounts at round numbers. The majority of the goals are small, at less than € 2,500. For larger amounts, the distribution has evident bunching at € 1,000, € 2,000, € 3,000, € 5,000, € 10,000, € 20,000, and € 50,000.
  The bottom-left panel of Figure3focuses on the 14 mutual funds from which users can choose to invest their savings. We classify the funds into three categories based on their type: Fixed Income, Balanced, and Equity—see TableIA.Ifor details. The majority of investors (55%) choose balanced funds, followed by fixed-income and equity funds selected by 32% and 13% of the users, respectively. Individuals avoid exposure to pure equity funds, suggesting a relatively high degree of risk aversion and low trust in the stock market (Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2008)).
  The bottom-right panel of Figure3focuses on goal categories. Half of the individuals set goals that do not fall naturally into specific categories and are assigned to a generic Savings category. Turning to the other categories, Travel is the most common, comprising 20% of the targets, followed by Car, Hobby, and House, each making up a little less than 10% of the categories.
  We start by depicting the relation between the risk of the investment vehicles chosen by the investors (measured as the annualized standard deviation of returns) and the three most important goal characteristics associated with them: horizon, amount, and category. Panels A and B of Figure4focus on horizon and amount, respectively. To capture possible nonlinearities, we use a nearest-neighbor estimator. As the goal horizon increases, users choose riskier funds, supporting the idea that they believe stocks are a better investment for the long run than fixed-income securities (Siegel (1998)). Similarly, goals with higher amounts are associated with riskier funds. However, the gradient of fund risk with respect to goal amount is smaller than the gradient of fund risk with respect to goal horizon.
  The bottom panels focus on goal categories: Car, Hobby, House, Other, Savings, and Travel. Panel C displays the average risk (and associated 95% confidence interval) for each category. The spread between the category with the highest risk, Savings (6.9%), and the one with the lowest risk, Travel (6.2%), is only 0.7%. Moreover, the remaining categories are not associated with significantly different levels of risk as their confidence intervals are largely overlapping. Panel D displays the category composition for each type of fund (Fixed Income, Balanced, and Equity). Goal categories are equally distributed across fund types, with the exception of Savings having a greater presence in Equity funds at the expense of Travel. These results indicate that the association between goal category and risk is tenuous.
  Panel A of TableIIreports the results across four specifications. In the first, we exclude the interaction terms and only include goal and user characteristics. The coefficient estimates show a positive and statistically significant relationship between fund risk and both goal horizon and user age. Economically, a one-standard-deviation increase in goal horizon (user age) is associated with a 0.36% (0.53%) increase in the annualized return standard deviation of the funds selected. The coefficient on the female dummy is negative and significant confirming the well-established fact that females prefer safer investments because they are more risk averse. Females choose funds that are 0.55% less risky than those chosen by males. Finally, goal amount displays a positive but statistically insignificant relation with risk. In the second and third columns, we interact goal horizon and amount with gender and age, respectively. The interaction terms including goal horizon are statistically significant at the 5% level or better. Economically, females take almost double the risk of males as the investment horizon of their goal increases.14On the other hand, the relation between horizon and risk-taking weakens for older users. Finally, the interaction terms including goal amount are either statistically insignificant or economically small. These findings are confirmed in the last specification (column (4)), which contains the complete set of regressors.
  The results in Panel A could be driven in part by the distinctive volatility behavior of the different fund types over our sample period. Moreover, when choosing their investment vehicle, individuals are given the choice of prudent, dynamic, and aggressive funds that correspond to Fixed Income, Balanced, and Equity funds. Because users might select an investment vehicle based on its category rather than its level of risk, we reestimate equation (1) using three distinct logit models where the dependent variable is alternatively a dummy variable equal to one if the type of fund associated with the goal is a Fixed-income, Balanced, or Equity fund and zero otherwise.
  The results, reported in Panel B of TableII, confirm those obtained with fund risk as the main dependent variable. Coefficients for horizon and age turn from negative to increasingly positive moving from Fixed-income to Balanced and Equity funds, and are always significant at the 5% level or better. The opposite patterns emerge for the gender dummy while goal horizon is only significant for Balanced funds. This indicates that as the goal horizon increases, app users are less likely to invest in bond funds and more likely to invest in Balanced and Equity funds. Older users (female) are less (more) likely to invest in bond funds, while goal amount does not display a significant relation with risk. The interaction terms involving horizon are again strongly significant, indicating that females are more likely than males, and older users are less likely than younger users, to move into Balanced and Equity funds as their investment horizon increases. Finally, the interaction terms involving goal amount are statistically insignificant or imply negligible economic magnitudes.
  This section explores the dynamics of goal-setting behavior along two dimensions. We first explore the dynamics of goal creation and its persistence, that is, whether it is the same people always setting (and completing) goals or whether goal setting is widespread among all users (SectionIII.C.1). We then analyze how individuals dynamically deposit money on the app to attain their saving goals (SectionIII.C.2). Finally, we study the determinants of goal achievement in SectionIII.C.3.
  The practice of setting saving goals is quite widespread among users. Of the users who opened an account after the goal-setting feature was introduced, 41% set a goal. Among the goal setters, the median user sets one goal. Only 7% of users set more than one goal at a given time. These results suggest that people do not typically set multiple goals simultaneously, but prefer to instead focus their saving efforts on one target at a time.
  Figure5, Panel (A), displays the dynamics of goal creation. For each user, we only consider the sample starting in October 2017—just after goals were introduced. We then standardize the length of time that users are enrolled in the app from zero to one and plot the percentage of individuals who set at least one goal since starting to use the app (solid blue line) and the percentage of individuals who have at least one goal open at a particular time (blue dashed line).
  Individuals are slightly more likely to set goals just after enrolling in the app: The steepness of the blue line decreases, moving from zero to one along thex$x$-axis.15The endpoint of this line shows that 41% of the individuals on the app have set at least one goal by the time they stop using the app. The difference between the solid line and the dashed line measures the percentage of individuals who have stopped using goals at some point. The plot shows that this percentage amounts to only 14.6% of the goal setters (6%/41%), indicating that once individuals start setting goals, they do not stop using them.
  The solid and dashed green lines repeat this exercise but for users with at least two goals at every point in time. Two observations emerge. First, only 7% of the app users set two or more goals. Second, the probability of setting up goals is uniformly distributed: The steepness of the green line is almost constant as we move from zero to one along thex$x$-axis. The difference between the green dashed line and the solid line shows that 29% (2%/7%) of the users who set two or more goals stop doing it while using the app.
  To examine how individuals dynamically deposit money on the app to attain their saving goals, we first restrict the sample to the 14,521 goals with a deadline before the date we obtained the data (November 2, 2019). To ease the comparison across goals with different characteristics, we normalize to one both the horizon and the amount of each goal. For each goal, we then cumulate the amount deposited by the user over time so that we have the percentage of the goal completed as a function of the percentage of the time elapsed. Finally, we compute semiparametric estimates of the average percentage completion across all goals as a function of the time elapsed.
  Panel (B) of Figure5shows the results. The figure shows results both pooling across all goals (in black) and separating attained goals (in blue) from unattained goals (in green).16While difficult to discern, 95% confidence intervals are also indicated with dashed lines. As can be seen, unattained goals are very different from attained goals from their very inception. In the former case, individuals undersave from the very beginning—the green line is always below the red 45-degree line—while, in the latter case, individuals oversave, if anything, in the first part of the goal's life. Individuals oversave up to the 40thpercentile of the time duration of the goal and then progressively decrease their savings rate thereafter, only to increase it at the very end as needed to achieve the goal.
  We conclude this section by analyzing the determinants of goal achievement, that is, which investor and goal characteristics better predict goal achievement. Of the 14,521 goals that investors created, 4,088 (28.15%) were attained within the deadline, 3,568 (24.57%) were closed and not achieved, and 6,865 (47.28%) were overdue but still active.17
  The results are reported in TableIII. Column (1) reports coefficients for a specification that includes only goal categories. All the specific categories have a probability of goal achievement significantly lower than the generic savings category (the base category). Economically, the magnitudes are quite large. For example, a coefficient of −0.45 on “Hobby,” implies an odds ratio ofe−0.45=0.64$e^{-0.45}=0.64$—a 36% reduction in the relative probability of achieving a goal.
  Columns (2) and (3) use the goal horizon and the size of the goal as regressors in univariate specifications. In both cases, the coefficient is negative and significant—the shorter the horizon and the smaller the goal, the greater the completion probability. Column (4) includes only user characteristics. Men are significantly less likely to achieve their goals, possibly because they are overconfident and less responsible than females. Older users are more likely to achieve their goals, possibly because they are less financially constrained, more experienced in setting realistic goals, and/or more responsible with their finances.
  The last column includes all covariates at once. We do not observe significant changes in the coefficient estimates, which remain qualitatively unchanged. Overall, the results in this section suggest that goal and user characteristics are equally important to explain cross-sectional variation in goal completion.
article.IV. The Effect of Goal Setting on Saving:
Results from the psychology literature (Locke and Latham (2002), Locke and Latham (2006)) suggest that goal setting should have a positive effect on saving since setting goals allows individuals to remain focused on the saving objective and deadline, and allows them to track their performance against it. In addition, several theoretical models in the economics literature (Hsiaw (2013), Bénabou and Tirole (2004), Beshears, Milkman, and Schwartzstein (2016)) predict that individuals set goals to overcome their self-control problems. Such goals provide internal motivation because individuals derive utility from comparing outcomes to goals.
  However, prior works also show that goals can be counterproductive and have a discouraging effect if they are too ambitious and unrealistic—see, for example, John (2020). This concern is particularly relevant in our setting as individuals choose their goal characteristics, such as horizon and amount, and the goals that we study are nonbinding commitments.
  Our setting allows us to identify causal effects using a quasi-natural experiment. The app developers activated the goal-setting feature for 122 randomly chosen individuals (beta testers) 50 days before making it available to the overall population to ensure the reliability of the new version of the app. These users were unaware of being selected for the trial and were not volunteers. Consistent with this, only 28.4% of them set goals in the beta-testing period. The main difference between the procedure implemented by the firm and a standard randomized control trial is that the number of treated individuals is smaller than we would have preferred. Also, the company did not store details of the formal randomization scheme used. For example, we do not know if they stratified the sample. The 50-day trial duration is another limitation. Nevertheless, this quasi-natural experiment allows us to estimate a difference-in-differences model in which the beta testers are the treated group and the remaining users are the control group.
  Because we estimate our regression at the monthly level and we seek to be as comprehensive as possible, we estimate two specifications. In the first, theTreatedi,t$Treated_{i,t}$dummy is equal to one only for August 2017. In the second, it is equal to one for both August 2017 and September 2017. In both cases, we estimate our results using an event window that starts in August 2016. Having a full year of deposit observations before the treatment allays seasonality concerns in our estimated coefficients.
  Theβ$\beta$coefficient in equation (3) identifies an ITT effect because the beta testers are given access to the goal-setting technology but they are not required to use it. The coefficient therefore indicates whether goal setting would increase savings if it were deployed to the broader population at large.
  The results are reported in Panel A of TableIV. Irrespective of how the treatment variable is defined—August 2017 or August and September 2017—we find positive and statistically significant estimates. In particular, those who had access to the goal-setting features of the app saved on average an additional € 16.92 to € 20.01 per month relative to the rest of the users.
  To obtain the LATE effect, that is, the effect of goal setting for those who actually set goals, we divide the ITT by the percentage of beta testers who engaged in goal setting during the beta-testing period. For specification (2), the percentage of compliers is 28.4%, giving an LATE effect of € 16.92/28.4% = € 59.57 per month.
  Since the beta testers were unaware of being selected for the trial and were not volunteers, the strict exogeneity assumption is satisfied. The Stable Unit of Treatment Assumption (SUTVA) is also not a concern in our setting, because beta testers' actions are not directly observable by other users. Moreover, informal communications between the two groups are unlikely to occur, given that users are scattered throughout the Italian territory. The third key assumption underlying our difference-in-differences identification strategy is that the behavior of the beta testers and nonbeta testers would have followed similar dynamics absent the exogenous introduction of goal setting for the beta testers—the so-called parallel trends assumption. Following D'Acunto and Rossi (2022) and Lindner and McConnell (2019), we provide graphical evidence and estimate formal regression-based tests for parallel trends. Specifically, we estimate the specification:yi,t=πi+ρ0·t+ρ1·t·Di+εi,t$y_{i,t}=\pi _i+\rho _0\cdot t +\rho _1 \cdot t \cdot D_i+\epsilon _{i,t}$, whereyi,t$y_{i,t}$is the economic variable of interest,πi$\pi _i$represents user fixed effects,ρ0$\rho _0$is a coefficient for the slope for the non beta testers with respect to a time trend, andρ1$\rho _1$is a coefficient for the difference in slopes with respect to a time trend for the beta testers and the nonbeta testers. The null hypothesis of parallel trends corresponds toρ1=0$\rho _1=0$.
  In Panel (A) of Figure6, we display the time-series behavior of monthly net deposits for both the beta testers (in blue) and the rest of the app users (in red). Net deposits display some degree of cyclicality, and beta testers tend to save more than nonbeta testers, but the time-series behavior is similar across the two groups. The formal parallel trends test confirms the graphical results sinceρ1$\rho _1$is statistically insignificant (t$t$-statistic = 1.41).
  Panel (B) focuses on the number of monthly transactions on the app as a measure of overall user activity. Similar to the results for net deposits, beta testers are generally more active than nonbeta testers, but they display very similar time-series behavior. Once again, the parallel trends test estimates a statistically insignificantρ1$\rho _1$(t$t$-statistic = 0.81).
  Panel (C) focuses on app users' portfolio risk, measured as the annualized volatility of the mutual funds chosen. Beta testers choose riskier investments than the rest of the users, but we do not observe differential changes over time across the two groups. The same is true for the number of accounts reported in Panel (D). In both cases, the coefficient estimates forρ1$\rho _1$are statistically insignificant, with at$t$-statistic equal to 1.28 and −0.01, respectively.
  While the parallel trends assumption seems to hold in our setting, it ultimately remains an untestable assumption of the difference-in-differences estimator. For robustness, we follow Heckman, Ichimura, and Todd (1997), Heckman et al. (1998), Abadie (2005), Lindner and McConnell (2019), and Chabé-Ferret (2017), and combine difference-in-differences with matching.
  Before providing such estimates, we analyze the differences between beta testers and nonbeta testers in TableV. To capture users' activity, we use the following variables:Tenure$Tenure$, the number of months elapsed from the opening of user's first account to July 2017 (right before the beginning of the beta-testing period), andN.Accounts$N.\nobreakspace Accounts$andNetInflow$Net\nobreakspace {\it{Inflow}} $, the number of accounts opened and the monthly average net deposits into the app, computed from August 2016 to July 2017. To capture user risk preferences and demographic characteristics, we useRisk$Risk$, the average annual standard deviation across the funds used by the user,Gender$Gender$, a dummy variable equal to one if male, andAge$Age$, measured as of July 2017. Panel A displays the characteristics of the beta testers while Panel B focuses on the rest of the users in the sample. For this second sample, we also report thet$t$-statistics associated with the tests of the equality of means between beta testers and nonbeta testers. The results indicate that the two groups of users are rather similar. At the 5% significance level, beta testers and nonbeta testers are indistinguishable in terms of all characteristics except forTenure$Tenure$. Beta testers are statistically more experienced in that they have been on the app longer (516 months against 431). To account for such differences and create a sample of users more comparable with the beta testers, we use a nearest-neighbor propensity-score matching procedure. For each beta tester, we choose the closest five matches based on the six variables described above. Panel C shows the characteristics of the matched sample of users. All of thet$t$-tests are insignificant, indicating that these matched users are indeed statistically indistinguishable from the beta testers.
  As a final metric of comparison, we compare the adoption rate of goal setting across beta testers, the rest of the app users, and the matched users. Recall that 28% of the beta testers adopt goal setting in the first two months after the app's introduction. When we compute the corresponding percentage on the entire app user population, we find an adoption rate of 22%. This is lower but in line with results in TableVthat show that beta testers tend to be more active than the average app user. When we compute the corresponding percentage on the matched sample rather than the overall population, we find that it is equal to 24%, consistent with the matching procedure selecting individuals who are more active and more similar to the beta testers.
  Even though the results reported above do not highlight major differences between beta testers and nonbeta testers, in Panel B of TableIV, we reestimate the difference-in-differences specification in equation (3) using variations of the matched sample reported above. In the first two columns of Panel B, we follow the recommendation of Chabé-Ferret (2017), who argues that difference-in-differences with matching on pretreatment outcomes could bias the estimated coefficients. For these estimates, we construct our matched sample using all variables with the exclusion ofNetInflow$Net\nobreakspace {\it{Inflow}} $. This specification returns coefficient estimates very similar to those obtained on the full sample of app users (Panel A), ranging between 19.24 and 22.49. The statistical significance of the coefficients is remarkable, considering that our matched sample in Panel B is less than 10% of the size as the one in Panel A.
  Columns (3) and (4) report the results when we include the variableNetInflow$Net\nobreakspace {\it{Inflow}}$in the matching procedure. Once again, the coefficients are very similar in terms of magnitude (24.57 and 22.09) and statistical significance. Turning to columns (5) and (6), we again follow Chabé-Ferret (2017) and leave a gap between the matching period and the treatment period. In particular, we construct the matched sample as in columns (3) and (4) but use the period from August 2016 through May 2017 and then include aPlacebo Treateddummy that equals one for the beta testers group over the period June and July 2017. The results again point to the effectiveness of goal setting, as the coefficient estimates on the treated dummy equal 21.16 and 21.66, both statistically significant at the 1% level. The coefficient on thePlacebo Treateddummy is instead both statistically insignificant and economically close to zero.
  To provide further evidence on the validity of our identification strategy, we perform an additional placebo test altering the timing of the treatment. Specifically, we reestimate equation (3) using two pseudo treatment windows in the immediate proximity of the true one (i.e., August and September 2017). The first pseudo window consists of the two months before the beginning of the true treatment—June and July 2017—while the estimation window ranges from June 2016 to July 2017. For this specification, we expect theTreateddummy to be insignificant since beta testers could not access the goal-setting feature. The second pseudo window consists of the two months after the end of the true treatment period—October and November 2017—while the estimation window ranges from October 2016 to November 2017. For this specification, we expect theTreateddummy to be insignificant since both the beta testers and the rest of the users had access to the goal-setting feature. In both cases, the estimate of theTreateddummy is economically small (−2 and 4.5, respectively) and statistically insignificant (t$t$-statistic equal to −0.24 and 0.37, respectively).
article.V. Further Evidence on Goal Setting and:
While our difference-in-differences setting helps establish causality, it provides limited insights into the dynamics of goal setting and savings because the beta-testing period spanned only two months. Moreover, the number of beta testers does not provide enough power to explore heterogeneity across users' characteristics.
  In this section, we provide more comprehensive results that focus on the full cross-section and time series of app users. First, we study the relation between goals and saving behavior using panel regressions at the user level (SectionV.A). Second, we study heterogeneity across user-level characteristics that the literature has found to be associated with a lower propensity to save (SectionV.B). Third, we rule out substitution effects, whereby the increased savings that we observe inside the app crowd out the savings outside the app (SectionV.C). Finally, we assess whether the increase in saving associated with goal setting is economically large relative to users' income (SectionV.D).
  The coefficient of interest isβ$\beta$, which identifies the change in monthly savings when users engage in goal setting, controlling for individual-specific characteristics and time effects. The results, reported in column (1) of TableVI, indicate a large effect of goal setting on saving behavior. The coefficient equals 28.74, with at$t$-statistic of 16.37. The average saving across all users when not setting goals equals € 28.75. Thus, users double their savings when they set goals. Economically, these effects are remarkable given that the app did not include reminders, notifications, automatic saving plans, or other saving nudges during our sample period. Moreover, these quantities are rather similar to the difference-in-differences causal estimates in TableIV.
  In columns (2) and (3) of TableVI, we repeat the analysis on users' deposits and withdrawals, respectively. The coefficient onTarget_Dummyi,t$Target\_Dummy_{i,t}$suggests that those who set goals save an additional € 34.56 when setting goals, compared to when they do not. The effect of goal setting is less pronounced for withdrawals. Goal setters withdraw an additional € 5.60 compared to when they do not set goals, but the economic magnitude of the effect is rather small. By comparing the results for net deposits, deposits, and withdrawals, it is clear that goal setters deposited more in their accounts and also withdrew more. The additional withdrawals, however, are only a fraction of the additional deposits. As a result, the change in net deposits is strongly positive and significant.18
  The only difference with respect to equation (4) is in the definition ofTarget$Target$_Dummyi,s,t$Dummy_{i,s,t}$, which equals one only if individuali$i$used goal setting for thes$s$thmonth in the calendar montht$t$. In the specification above,s$s$ranges from −6 to 18 and denotes the event time relative to the initiation of the saving goal. Theβs$\beta _s$coefficients in specification (5) therefore decompose the effect ofβ$\beta$in specification (4). The coefficient estimates, shown in Figure7, suggest that the effect is strongest over the first six to eight months, peaking at € 45 per month before decreasing and asymptoting to approximately € 10 to 20 per month. This pattern suggests that initial enthusiasm explains part of the increase in savings, but not the full saving differential.19
  The literature identifies several factors that can explain individuals' undersaving. These explanations include low levels of financial literacy (Lusardi (2008), Lusardi and Mitchell (2014)), high impatience Laibson (1997), low levels of overall education (Bernheim, Garrett, and Maki (2001), Cole, Paulson, and Shastry (2014)), low income and socioeconomic status (Banerjee and Mullainathan (2010), Duflo and Banerjee (2011), Kuhnen and Miu (2017)), and user inattention (Karlan et al. (2016) and Gargano and Rossi (2018)). Therefore, it is natural to ask whether goal setting can help the users who the literature has identified as having the lowest propensity to save.
  The coefficient of interest in all of our specifications isβ$\beta$, which estimates the effect of goal setting on those individuals who are at the highest risk of undersaving according to the literature. Theγ$\gamma$coefficient instead estimates the differential effect of goal setting on those individuals who the literature identifies as high savers compared to low savers.
  Given that five of the six conditioning variables are available only for a subset of users who complete the survey, the first column does not include any interaction and reestimates the specification in column (1) of TableVIon the sample of survey respondents. The results on this subsample are very similar to those computed on the full sample of app users, allaying concerns of sample selection biases in the population of survey respondents.
  Moving to the remaining specifications, across columns (2) through (6), theβ$\beta$coefficients show that even those who are more prone to undersaving increase their saving significantly after engaging in goal setting. If anything, in the majority of instances, we find that goal setting has the same effect across all users. This can be seen in columns (3) through (6), where theγ$\gamma$coefficient is statistically insignificant, suggesting there is no differential increase in saving associated with goal setting for the individuals with high patience, high education, high income, and high attention. Financial literacy is the only conditioning variable for which we find a statistically significantγ$\gamma$coefficient. The interaction between financial literacy and goal setting is estimated to increase the effect of goal setting by 19.82/31.94 = 62%, which is economically significant and suggests that FinTech apps could be deployed in conjunction with financial literacy to amplify their effectiveness.
  Overall, these results suggest that goal setting can be used to help the portion of the population at the highest risk of undersaving.
  A possible concern with our results is that individuals could perceive saving within and outside the app as substitutes and therefore decrease their savings outside the app when they start using it. Unfortunately, we cannot rule out this mechanism with administrative data only, as we do not have information on deposits and withdrawals on accounts outside the app.
  To circumvent this limitation, we exploit three survey questions related to individuals' savings outside the app, before and after its adoption. The first asks users whether they increased, decreased, or left unchanged their savings rate outside the app after its adoption. The results are provided in Panel A of Figure8. The majority of the respondents (51.2%) say they have not changed their savings outside the app. A significant number (35.0%) state that they increased their savings outside the app. Finally, only a small minority of individuals (13.8%) state they decreased their savings outside the app.
  In the next two questions, the survey explicitly asks the respondents for their saving rates before and after adopting the app and gave them five options to choose from: (1) € 0, (2) € 1 to € 49, (3) € 50 to € 100, (4) € 101 to € 150, and (5) € 150+. As shown in Panel B of Figure8, individuals increased their savings outside the app after its adoption. The red bars—denoting saving after adoption—are higher than the blue bars—denoting saving before adoption—for all response categories except the € 0 option. Thus, the entire distribution of saving outside the app increases after individuals sign up, ruling out a potential substitution effect between saving inside and outside the app.
  We perform several additional quantitative tests to rule out the possibility of a substitution effect between saving inside and outside the app after adopting goal setting. We report the results in TableIA.II. In the first column, we reestimate regression specification (4) on the survey respondents only. We find a very similar effect as on the full sample (column (1) of TableVI). In the second column, we restrict the sample to those survey respondents who state they have no savings outside the app. The results are virtually identical.
  In the last three specifications, we interact the target adoption dummy with three groups of users. The first comprises users who do not save outside the app. For these users, the substitution channel cannot be present by construction, and thus, we expect a lower increase in saving after adopting the target (negative interaction coefficient) if a substitution effect was in place among the rest of the app users. The second group comprises users who state that they do not change their saving rate outside the app after its adoption. For these users, the substitution effect should be zero and we should see a negative interaction coefficient. Finally, the third group comprises users who state that they reduce their savings outside the app after its adoption. These users should experience the greatest substitution effect, compared to the rest of the users, and thus, we expect a positive and significant interaction coefficient. In all cases, we find that the coefficients on the interactions are economically small and statistically insignificant, suggesting there is no evidence of substitution between saving within and outside the app in our data.
  An additional concern could be that individuals increased their debt levels—particularly credit card debt—after increasing their savings on the app as a result of goal setting. This is unlikely to be the case for two reasons. First, recent studies show that individuals who increase savings as a result of saving nudges do not subsequently increase their debt balances (Medina and Pagel (2021), Beshears et al. (2022)). Second, the use of credit cards is much less widespread in Italy, compared to the United States. According to the Euromonitor International Consumer Finance 2019 report, only 26% of the households hold a credit card in Italy. The corresponding percentages for the rest of Western Europe and the United States are, respectively, 45% and 69%.
  This last section quantifies the economic effects of goal setting in boosting savings for the subset of app users who participate in the survey and provide monthly income information. This allows us to compute the saving rate for each individual as a percentage of her net income.20We construct, first, individuals' saving rate before adopting goal setting; second, the implied saving rate associated with saving goals; and third, the saving rate users achieve when they use goal setting.
  The difference between the saving rate implied by goals and the saving rate when not setting goals can be thought of as the potential effect of goal setting in increasing savings rates. The difference between the savings rate with goals and the savings rate without goals captures the realized effect of goal setting. Finally, the difference between the saving rate implied by the goals and the savings rate obtained with goal setting can have different interpretations depending on investors' assumed behavior. If we assume that individuals are overconfident, the quantity measures the degree to which investors underestimate the difficulties they face in saving. If we assume that individuals set intentionally overly optimistic goals, the quantity measures how much individuals have to overcommit to achieve their desired saving rates. If we rule out overconfidence and overcommitment, the difference can be interpreted as the leakage associated with soft goals.
  We depict these relations in the following diagram:
  
  The median individual saves 1% of her net income when she does not set goals. The planned percentage savings associated with goals equals 4.6% of her net income. Finally, the percentage savings realized when setting goals equals 2.4% of her income. We obtain virtually identical values when we restrict the analysis to only those individuals who do not save anything outside the app: 1%, 6%, and 2%, respectively.21Irrespective of the economic interpretation, these differences in savings rates highlight that goal setting helps individuals increase their baseline savings rate.
  Economically, the effects are rather large. Take an individual who invests in an all-equity mutual fund and therefore can expect a 6% annual risk premium. This individual would save one month's worth of her net income in 55 months (almost five years). Under the savings rate implied by the saving plan (4.6%), the same individual would achieve the same overall savings in 19 months (a little less than two years). Finally, if she was engaged in goal setting, she would achieve that overall savings level in 33 months (a little less than three years).
article.VI. Why Does Goal Setting Increase Savin:
The psychology and economics literatures suggest several channels through which goal setting can increase savings. One possible channel is “concreteness,” whereby goals can boost savings due to mental accounting because the specific purpose of a goal creates a specific segregated mental account that acts as a saving motivator (Thaler (1985), Shefrin and Thaler (1988), Thaler (1990), Thaler (1999)). A large literature in psychology also demonstrates that setting explicit and concrete goals increases achievement, even in the absence of external incentives Locke and Latham (1990). Another possible channel is that of attention/monitoring, which implies that savings should increase with goal setting because individuals experience disutility from falling short of their goal (Bénabou and Tirole (2004), Hsiaw (2013)) and/or goals help individuals to follow through on their intentions by increasing their attention (Karlan et al. (2016)).
  Because we cannot fully disentangle the different channels discussed above, in this section, we conduct two empirical exercises to explore the relative merits of these mechanisms. In SectionVI.B, we provide survey evidence on the most motivating aspects of goal setting and on the factors users believe to be most responsible for their success or failure in reaching their goals. In SectionVI.B, we use administrative data to study how goal type relates to saving behavior.
  In Figure9, Panel A, we start by providing the distribution of responses to the question“In your experience, what is the most motivating aspect of having a saving goal?”The options provided emphasized three distinct aspects of goals. The first two related to the deadline dimension and the ability to track one's progress toward goals. The third related to concreteness or the purpose of the goal. The survey provided users with the following choices: (1) having a deadline, (2) the ability to monitor my progress towards the goal, (3) the purpose of the goal, (4) other, and (5) I do not think having a goal is motivating.
  The majority of app users (57%) respond that the ability to monitor their progress toward the goal was the most motivating aspect, followed by the purpose of the goal (28.5%) and having a deadline (10.4%). We corroborate these results using observational data on individuals' login activity on the app, before and after setting goals. Specifically, we estimate a regression identical to specification (4) except that we replace the monthly net deposit dependent variable with the (logged) number of monthly logins on the app. Individuals increase their login activity on the app by 6% when they set goals, consistent with the intuition that goal setting increases the amount of time and energy people spend thinking about their savings and with the idea that goals are effective because they motivate investors to pay more attention to their savings.
  In Panel B and C of Figure9, we summarize results on which factors app users believe have determined the success or failure of their goals. When asked about their attained goals, 18% of the app users indicate that the purpose of the goal was the most decisive factor, while 54% of respondents indicate that fund returns and the ability to monitor their progress toward their goal played a more important role. When asked about the goals they have not attained, a considerable fraction of respondents (27.1%) are unable to provide a precise answer and choose the “Do not know” option. Of the remaining options, the most common is “unexpected expenses” (30%) followed by a too ambitious goal (15.8%), poor fund returns (9%), and lost motivation (7%).
  Overall, the results in this section suggest that multiple mechanisms are at play, with greater emphasis on a monitoring channel compared to a goal specificity channel.
  The first column of TableVIIIshows economically small effects related to the type of target. All interactions are negative but only those for the “leisure” and “other” categories are statistically significant. Economically the effects are negative but not very large, suggesting that the specific purpose of the target does not affect users' saving behavior.
  A potential source of concern is that users might select the “savings” category but still have a specific purpose in mind. For example, they may be thinking about saving for an emergency fund.22To rule out such cases, we use the textual descriptions of the goals supplied by the users at the time of goal creation, and generate a restrictive “Saving” category that includes only goals described solely by the word “saving.” These are the goals with the lowest likelihood of being specific in nature. The results, displayed in column (2), are very similar to those in column (1).
  A second source of error could come from the fact that, after describing the goal, users might misclassify the category it belongs to (i.e., may select the Savings category even if the target's name is “Trip to New York”). We use data from the text entered by the users to describe the goals and manually recategorize them into 12 categories: Hobby, Travel, Other, Vehicle, House, Celebrations, Consumer Goods, Entrepreneurship, Family, Liability, Health, and Savings. We describe the procedure in theInternet Appendix. We run several tests to rule out the possibility that this source of error affects our results. In column (3), we remove from the Savings category all goals with user descriptions that suggest they belong to one of the alternative categories. The results are virtually unchanged. In column (4), we directly use the 12 categories constructed from users' goal descriptions as individual dummies. More specifically, we construct interactions that range from one to 11 (Interaction_1$Interaction\_1$throughInteraction_11$Interaction\_11$) that correspond to the category order reported above (e.g., Hobby corresponds toInteraction_1$Interaction\_1$and Health toInteraction_11$Interaction\_11$). Even in this case, all specific goal categories have either negative and significant or insignificant coefficients. The only exception is Liability, which has a positive coefficient that is barely significant at the 10% level (t$t$-statistic = 1.70). Finally, in column (5), we take the 12 categories that we construct from the goal descriptions and map them into the leisure category, containing Hobby and Travel, the durable category, containingVehicleandHouse, and other, containing all the other goal types. In all cases, the results are consistent with those reported in column (1).
  Column (6) focuses on the horizon of the goal andInteraction_1i$Interaction\_1_{i}$identifies targets with horizons of less than (or equal to) a year. We therefore take targets with horizons greater than one year as the base case. Individuals save significantly more when they set targets with short horizons: The coefficient on the short-horizon interaction is positive and statistically significant, but the effect is economically small.
  Column (7) focuses on the amount of the goal andInteraction_1i$Interaction\_1_{i}$identifies targets smaller than € 1,000, taking targets with horizons greater than € 1,000 as the base case. We do not find any relation between the size of the target and saving behavior.
  Overall, the results in this section show that goal concreteness may not be a fundamentally important channel when it comes to savings goals.
article.VII. Conclusions:
We study the effectiveness of soft goals in saving decisions using data from a saving FinTech app. A large percentage of the app users set goals in a persistent fashion and tend to set one goal at a time rather than have multiple simultaneous goals. The typical goal has a short horizon, is small in monetary terms, is more likely to have a general rather than a specific purpose, and is more likely to be associated with Balanced rather than pure Equity or Fixed Income funds.
  Using a difference-in-differences identification strategy that exploits the random assignment of users into a group of beta testers who can set goals and a group of users who cannot, we document that setting goals increases individuals' saving rate and that the effect is causal. We also show that the increased savings within the app do not come at the expense of reduced savings outside the app and that goal setting is also very effective for the part of the population at greater risk of undersaving.
  We complement the administrative app data with a survey administered to a sample of app users and highlight that multiple economic mechanisms are at play in explaining the effectiveness of goal setting, with greater emphasis on a monitoring channel compared to a goal specificity channel.
  Taken together, our findings indicate that soft goals have an overall positive effect on saving. This is reassuring, given that a large number of FinTech firms and large financial institutions have been introducing these tools in recent years. In addition, given that mobile phone usage is becoming widespread across developing countries—see Jack and Suri (2014) and Duflo and Banerjee (2011) for examples—the soft goals that we study in this paper could be deployed to help low-income individuals, who are arguably among those who could benefit from these FinTech tools the most.
